annotations.label: Clique

anchor.snippet: A clique in an undirected graph is a subset of its vertices that are all adjacent to each other

annotations.note: `IsClique s` is defined as `s.Pairwise G.Adj`, i.e. a set of pairwise-adjacent vertices.

annotations.label: Clique number

anchor.snippet: The clique number is the number of vertices in the largest clique

annotations.note: `cliqueNum G` is `sSup {n | ∃ s, G.IsNClique n s}`, the size of the largest clique.

annotations.label: Graph coloring

anchor.snippet: A graph coloring assigns a color to each vertex so that each two adjacent vertices have different colors

annotations.note: `Coloring α` is a homomorphism into the complete graph on `α`, equivalently a proper vertex coloring.

annotations.label: Chromatic number

anchor.snippet: The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colors in any coloring

annotations.note: `chromaticNumber G` is the least `n : ℕ∞` for which `G` is `n`-colorable.

annotations.label: Chromatic number at least clique number

anchor.snippet: the chromatic number is always greater than or equal to the clique number

annotations.note: `cliqueNum_le_chromaticNumber` proves `G.cliqueNum ≤ G.chromaticNumber`.

annotations.label: Perfect graph

anchor.snippet: The perfect graphs are defined as the graphs for which these two numbers are equal

annotations.note: There is no perfect-graph predicate in Mathlib (only `IsClique`, `cliqueNum`, and `chromaticNumber` building blocks).

annotations.label: Perfect graph theorem

anchor.snippet: The perfect graph theorem asserts that the complement graph of a perfect graph is itself perfect

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of perfect graph, so this theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Perfect graph (independence = clique cover)

anchor.snippet: they are the graphs for which, in each induced subgraph, the independence number equals the clique cover number

annotations.note: Neither a clique-cover number nor a perfect-graph definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Odd cycle is not perfect

anchor.snippet: A cycle of odd length, greater than three, is not perfect

annotations.note: Perfectness is not formalized, so this example cannot be stated.

annotations.label: Strong perfect graph theorem

anchor.snippet: The strong perfect graph theorem asserts that these are the only forbidden induced subgraphs for the perfect graphs

annotations.note: Mathlib has no perfect/Berge graph notion or this characterization.

annotations.label: Product characterization of perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: they are the graphs for which the product of the clique number and independence number is greater than or equal to the number of vertices

annotations.note: No perfect-graph characterization is present even though `cliqueNum` and `indepNum` exist.

annotations.label: Gallai: complement of bipartite graph is perfect

anchor.snippet: the complement of a bipartite graph is perfect

annotations.note: Bipartiteness and graph complement are formalized, but perfectness and this proposition are not.

annotations.label: Berge graphs

anchor.snippet: were called Berge graphs

annotations.note: Berge graphs (no odd hole/antihole) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bipartite graphs are perfect

anchor.snippet: bipartite graphs are perfect

annotations.note: `IsBipartite` (= `Colorable 2`) exists, but perfectness and this implication are not formalized.

annotations.label: Trees and median graphs

anchor.snippet: including for instance the trees and median graphs

annotations.note: `IsTree` is formalized, but median graphs and the perfect/bipartite claim are not.

annotations.label: Kőnig's theorem (matchings and vertex covers)

anchor.snippet: an equality between the size of the maximum matching and the minimum vertex cover in bipartite graphs, the usual formulation of Kőnig's theorem

annotations.note: `vertexCoverNum` and `Subgraph.IsMatching` are defined, but König's min-max equality is not proved.

annotations.label: Line graph and matching

anchor.snippet: is the same thing as an independent set in the line graph

annotations.note: `lineGraph`, `IsMatching`, and `IsIndepSet` all exist, but the matching↔line-graph-independent-set equivalence is not stated.

annotations.label: Line graphs of bipartite graphs are perfect

anchor.snippet: the line graphs of bipartite graphs are perfect

annotations.note: Line graphs and bipartiteness are formalized, but perfectness and this result are not.

annotations.label: Rook's graphs

anchor.snippet: Examples include the rook's graphs

annotations.note: Rook's graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line graph of bipartite graph in a rook's graph

anchor.snippet: Every line graph of a bipartite graph is an induced subgraph of a rook's graph

annotations.note: Rook's graphs are absent, so this embedding statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Kőnig: maximum degree equals chromatic index

anchor.snippet: The equality of maximum degree and chromatic index, in bipartite graphs, is another theorem of Dénes Kőnig

annotations.note: Mathlib has no edge coloring or chromatic index, so this theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Vizing's theorem

anchor.snippet: this is Vizing's theorem

annotations.note: Chromatic index/edge coloring is not in Mathlib, so Vizing's theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Line perfect graph

anchor.snippet: These are the graphs whose biconnected components are bipartite graphs

annotations.note: Line perfect graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Line perfect graphs are perfect

anchor.snippet: so every line perfect graph is perfect

annotations.note: Neither line perfect graphs nor perfectness are formalized.

annotations.label: Structural decomposition of perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: every perfect graph that is not already in one of these four classes can be decomposed by partitioning its vertices into subsets

annotations.note: The structure theorem for perfect graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Partially ordered set

anchor.snippet: A partially ordered set is defined by its set of elements, and a comparison relation

annotations.note: `PartialOrder` is the standard typeclass for a reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive order.

annotations.label: Comparable and incomparable elements

anchor.snippet: and incomparable otherwise

annotations.note: `IncompRel r a b := ¬ r a b ∧ ¬ r b a` formalizes incomparability (comparability is `SymmGen`/`a ≤ b ∨ b ≤ a`).

annotations.label: Comparability graph

anchor.snippet: The comparability graph of a partially ordered set has the set elements as its vertices

annotations.note: No construction of a comparability graph from a poset exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Incomparability graph

anchor.snippet: Its complement is called an incomparability graph

annotations.note: Incomparability graphs as `SimpleGraph` objects are not defined.

annotations.label: Comparability graphs are perfect

anchor.snippet: Finite comparability graphs (and their complementary incomparability graphs) are always perfect

annotations.note: Comparability graphs and perfectness are both absent.

annotations.label: Chain

anchor.snippet: such a subset is called a chain

annotations.note: `IsChain r s` says every two distinct elements of `s` are related, i.e. totally ordered.

annotations.label: Antichain

anchor.snippet: such a subset is called an antichain

annotations.note: `IsAntichain r s` says distinct elements of `s` are pairwise unrelated.

annotations.label: Dilworth's theorem

anchor.snippet: the size of the largest antichain equals the minimum number of chains into which the elements can be partitioned

annotations.note: Dilworth's theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mirsky's theorem

anchor.snippet: the size of the largest chain equals the minimum number of antichains into which the elements can be partitioned

annotations.note: Mirsky's theorem is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every bipartite graph is a comparability graph

anchor.snippet: Every bipartite graph is a comparability graph

annotations.note: Comparability graphs are not defined, so this statement cannot be expressed.

annotations.label: Permutation graph

anchor.snippet: A permutation graph is defined from a permutation on a totally ordered sequence of elements

annotations.note: Permutation graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characterization of permutation graphs

anchor.snippet: the permutation graphs are exactly the graphs that are both comparability and incomparability graphs

annotations.note: Neither permutation nor comparability graphs are formalized.

annotations.label: Erdős–Szekeres theorem

anchor.snippet: the special case of this inequality for permutation graphs is the Erdős–Szekeres theorem

annotations.note: Erdős–Szekeres is proved only in `Archive/Wiedijk100Theorems/AscendingDescendingSequences.lean`, not in `Mathlib/`.

annotations.label: Interval graphs

anchor.snippet: The interval graphs are the incomparability graphs of interval orders

annotations.note: Interval graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trapezoid graphs

anchor.snippet: Both interval graphs and permutation graphs are generalized by the trapezoid graphs

annotations.note: Trapezoid graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indifference graphs

anchor.snippet: the indifference graphs , the incomparability graphs of semiorders

annotations.note: Indifference graphs and semiorders are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trivially perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: produce trivially perfect graphs , the comparability graphs of ordered trees

annotations.note: Trivially perfect graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Independence number equals number of maximal cliques

anchor.snippet: In them, the independence number equals the number of maximal cliques

annotations.note: `indepNum` exists, but this equality about trivially perfect graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Split graph

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: A split graph is a graph that can be partitioned into a clique and an independent set

annotations.note: Split graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Split graphs are perfect

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: these graphs have equal clique numbers and chromatic numbers, and are perfect

annotations.note: Neither split graphs nor perfectness are formalized.

annotations.label: Unipolar graphs

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: the unipolar graphs can be partitioned into a clique and a cluster graph

annotations.note: Unipolar graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized split graphs

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: The unipolar graphs and their complements together form the class of generalized split graphs

annotations.note: Generalized split graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Almost all perfect graphs are generalized split graphs

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: Almost all perfect graphs are generalized split graphs

annotations.note: This asymptotic/random-graph result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Almost all perfect graphs are Hamiltonian

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: almost all perfect graphs contain a Hamiltonian cycle

annotations.note: `IsHamiltonian` exists, but this random-perfect-graph statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: Limiting induced-subgraph probability

anchor.section: Split graphs and random perfect graphs
anchor.snippet: occurs as an induced subgraph of a large random perfect graph is 0, 1/2, or 1

annotations.note: This limiting-probability result for random perfect graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Chordal graphs

anchor.snippet: The chordal graphs are the graphs formed by a construction of this type in which, at the time a vertex is added, its neighbors form a clique

annotations.note: Chordal graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chordal graphs have no holes

anchor.snippet: Chordal graphs may also be characterized as the graphs that have no holes (even or odd)

annotations.note: Chordal graphs and this characterization are not formalized.

annotations.label: Split graphs as chordal with chordal complement

anchor.snippet: The split graphs are exactly the graphs that are chordal and have a chordal complement

annotations.note: Neither chordal nor split graphs are formalized.

annotations.label: k-trees

anchor.snippet: are chordal graphs formed by starting with a

annotations.note: k-trees are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distance-hereditary graphs

anchor.snippet: The distance-hereditary graphs are formed, starting from a single-vertex graph, by repeatedly adding degree-one vertices

annotations.note: Distance-hereditary graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cograph

anchor.snippet: If only the twin operations are used, the result is a cograph

annotations.note: Cographs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cographs as comparability graphs of series-parallel orders

anchor.snippet: The cographs are the comparability graphs of series-parallel partial orders

annotations.note: Cographs, comparability graphs, and series-parallel orders are not formalized.

annotations.label: Ptolemaic graphs

anchor.snippet: The graphs that are both chordal and distance-hereditary are called Ptolemaic graphs

annotations.note: Ptolemaic graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Windmill and block graphs

anchor.snippet: They include as special cases the windmill graphs consisting of cliques joined at a single vertex, and the block graphs

annotations.note: Windmill and block graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Threshold graphs

anchor.snippet: The threshold graphs are formed from an empty graph by repeatedly adding either an isolated vertex

annotations.note: Threshold graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characterization of threshold graphs

anchor.snippet: They are exactly the graphs that are both trivially perfect and the complement of a trivially perfect graph

annotations.note: Threshold and trivially perfect graphs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Greedy coloring of chordal graphs is optimal

anchor.snippet: If the vertices of a chordal graph are colored in the order of an incremental construction sequence using a greedy coloring algorithm, the result will be an optimal coloring

annotations.note: Chordal graphs and greedy coloring are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfectly orderable graphs

anchor.snippet: the family of perfectly orderable graphs , the graphs for which there exists an ordering that

annotations.note: Perfectly orderable graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cographs and all vertex orderings

anchor.snippet: The cographs are exactly the graphs for which all vertex orderings have this property

annotations.note: Cographs and perfectly orderable graphs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Strongly perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: The strongly perfect graphs are graphs in which, in every induced subgraph, there exists an independent set that intersects all maximal cliques

annotations.note: Strongly perfect graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Meyniel graphs

anchor.snippet: In the Meyniel graphs or very strongly perfect graphs , every vertex belongs to such an independent set

annotations.note: Meyniel graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characterization of Meyniel graphs

anchor.snippet: The Meyniel graphs can also be characterized as the graphs in which every odd cycle of length five or more has at least two chords

annotations.note: Meyniel graphs and this characterization are not formalized.

annotations.label: Parity graph

anchor.snippet: A parity graph is defined by the property that between every two vertices, all induced paths have equal parity

annotations.note: Parity graphs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parity graphs are Meyniel and perfect

anchor.snippet: Parity graphs are Meyniel graphs, and therefore perfect

annotations.note: Parity graphs, Meyniel graphs, and perfectness are all absent.

annotations.label: Parity graphs and perfect prisms

anchor.snippet: the parity graphs are the only graphs whose prisms are perfect

annotations.note: Parity graphs, prisms, and perfectness are not formalized.

annotations.label: Complexity of linear and integer programming

anchor.snippet: linear programming can be solved in polynomial time , but integer programming is NP-hard

annotations.note: Mathlib has no computational-complexity framework (polynomial time, NP-hardness).

annotations.label: Integral linear program

anchor.snippet: The linear program is called an integral linear program if an optimal solution to the integer program is also optimal for the linear program

annotations.note: Integral linear programs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chvátal: matrices and perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: the maximal clique versus vertex incidence matrix of a perfect graph

annotations.note: This polyhedral characterization of perfect graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Coincidence of independent-set and clique polytopes

anchor.snippet: The perfect graphs are the only graphs for which the two polytopes defined in this way from independent sets and from maximal cliques coincide

annotations.note: Stable-set/clique polytopes for graphs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Polynomial-time problems on perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: In all perfect graphs, the graph coloring problem , maximum clique problem , and maximum independent set problem can all be solved in polynomial time

annotations.note: No complexity-theoretic results on graph algorithms exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lovász number

anchor.snippet: The Lovász number of any graph can be determined by labeling its vertices by high dimensional unit vectors

annotations.note: The Lovász number is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lovász number sandwich

anchor.snippet: the Lovász number is sandwiched between the chromatic number and clique number

annotations.note: The Lovász number and the sandwich theorem are not formalized.

annotations.label: Maximum clique algorithm

anchor.snippet: a maximum clique can be found by the following algorithm

annotations.note: `IsMaximumClique` exists, but no maximum-clique algorithm is formalized.

annotations.label: Polynomial-time recognition of perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: this can be used to test whether a given graph is perfect, in polynomial time

annotations.note: Perfect graphs and recognition algorithms are not formalized.

annotations.label: χ-bounded graph class

anchor.snippet: a graph class is said to be χ-bounded if the chromatic number of the graphs in the class can be bounded by a function of their clique number

annotations.note: χ-boundedness is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect graphs as identity χ-binding

anchor.snippet: The perfect graphs are exactly the graphs for which this function is the identity

annotations.note: Neither χ-bounding functions nor perfect graphs are formalized.

annotations.label: Domination perfect graphs

anchor.snippet: the domination perfect graphs are defined as graphs in which

annotations.note: Domination perfect graphs (and domination number) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Claw-free graphs

anchor.snippet: These include, for instance, the claw-free graphs

annotations.note: Claw-free graphs are not defined in Mathlib.
